alternatives.dialogicTeaching: How did you decide that the remainder of one should be written as one tenth?
alternatives.productiveFailure: Take a look at your divisor. If you have one left over, how can you use that divisor to figure out what fraction that remainder actually represents?
alternatives.uptake: One tenth? I see you’re trying to turn that remainder into a fraction—looking back at our divisor, how did you decide that the one should be out of ten?
alternatives.pressingForReasoning: How did you decide that the remainder of one should be written as one tenth?

context.speaker: teacher

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Then, please do so.  Now, shush.  You absolutely have to cut properly first.  [Cuts paper] Notice, sir, you’ve got a huge chunk you took out of this.  It’s gonna cause a really serious problem when you try to put it together.  But, that’s the way it goes.  Shush, shush.  You have to cut out each of these individual teeth, and you have to fold every one individually.

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Yeah, for real.   If this is gonna get put together, that’s the way we create a three dimensional cylinder.

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: All right.  Shush.  Well, I’m curious.  Shush.  Excuse  you.

context.speaker: student
context.text: Oh, poop.  Can I still try to do it this way?

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Well, you know what, I’m curious, no.  You may not.   It’s not gonna go together.  I can prove you wrong 100 percent of the time on that one.

alternatives.dialogicTeaching: I’m curious to see your approach. If you try it that way, how do you think those edges will connect to form the cylinder shape?
alternatives.productiveFailure: Go ahead and try your way first. As you’re building, pay close attention to where the side meets the base—see if you can find a way to make those edges stay together without the tabs.
alternatives.uptake: I’m interested to see how that might work—if you try it your way without those individual folds, how do you think that’s going to change the way the round part of the cylinder connects to the flat base?
alternatives.pressingForReasoning: Walk me through your thinking—how do you plan to get those edges to line up and stay together to form the cylinder if you do it that way?

context.text: All right, so you started in Boston, then to Orlando and up in Los Angeles.  So we get this together.  So now where are you gonna start a trip?  You’re gonna do another trip.  Do number two now.  Trip two.

What number did you start off at?  Yep. [Inaudible]  You haven’t gone anywhere, so there can be no more miles.  There can be no miles there if you haven’t gone anywhere.  So what number do you have to start at?

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Zero.  Now where are you going?

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: So you go to Dallas to Florida?  So show me.

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: How do you get to Florida from there?

alternatives.dialogicTeaching: Tell me more about that route—what do you notice about the path you're taking?
alternatives.productiveFailure: Try it out and see what happens—if you go that way, how will you check if the total miles still match your plan?

alternatives.pressingForReasoning: Tell me more about why you’re thinking of going that way—how would that path help you reach your destination?

context.text: [Inaudible] come and plot three, zero.  When I say your name. Student D plot three, zero.

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: All right, good job.  Yes.  So we started at zero.  Our first coordinate is three.  We moved to the right three times. One, two, three.  Our second coordinate is zero and we stay where we are.  Good job.  You plot your point, write your coordinates in parenthesis separated by a comma.  Now let’s see if we can take it a step further.  Let’s pretend this grid right here is actually a neighborhood and here’s where my house is.  Somebody raise your hand and tell me [inaudible] and tell me what’s the coordinates for my house?  Student K.

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: My house is at five, four.  So even when the point is already on the grid, you can still locate it because the same rules apply.  You start at zero, move across, one, two, three, four, five.  I’m on this spot.  I’m goin’ up four times, one, two, three, four.  So my house is at five, four.  My house is at five, four.  But let’s say I’m home.  Let’s say I’m home and I need to go to the store.  I don’t know how to draw a store but that’s a store.  Let’s say I’m home and I want to go to the store.

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: I need you to tell me how I get from my house to the store. Remember you always move horizontally first.  Tell me, do I need to go left, right how many times?  Do I need to go up, down and how many times?  Horizontally is always first.  So do I need to go left or right?  If so, how many times.  And do I need to go up or down and if so, how many times? Student M?

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: I have to so left two times, so one, two and then I go?

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Down two times, one two.  Now what is the coordinates for the store?

alternatives.dialogicTeaching: Walk me through how you got to two, two; if we start at five and move left two spots, what number are we standing on now?
alternatives.productiveFailure: Let’s test that out. If you plot the point (2, 2) on our grid, does it land exactly where we drew the store, or do we need to re-count our jumps from the house?
alternatives.uptake: You're picking up on those two jumps we made; if we started at five on the bottom and moved left two spaces, what number does that land us on for our first coordinate?
alternatives.pressingForReasoning: Can you explain how you found those coordinates based on where we landed on the grid?

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Yes, this is the part where you have to have your answer in your mouth before you raise your hand.  It’s got to be there ready to burst out the second I call on you.  Okay?

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Ready?  “Ramona likes to go hiking once a week.  Her favorite trail is 12 miles long.  She hiked that trail 52 times last year.  About how many total miles did Ramona hike?” Student S?

alternatives.dialogicTeaching: Wait, don’t say “never mind” just yet—can you walk us through how you arrived at 624?
alternatives.productiveFailure: Hold on, don't take it back just yet. What did you notice about the question that made you say "never mind"?
alternatives.uptake: You found the exact total of 624, so if we are looking for an estimate, how can that number help us figure out which numbers we should round to get close to that answer?
alternatives.pressingForReasoning: How did you arrive at 624, and how can that number help us figure out "about" how many miles she hiked?
